ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Dal Tadka; FOODON_03311434 (lentil dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Lentils, cooked) is the closest parent; no specific Dal Tadka entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Dal Tadka or a sufficiently close lentil dish preparation.

preparation_method: sautéed spices in ghee or oil
main_ingredients: yellow lentils (toor dal or chana dal)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Dal Tadka is approximately 250 g, typically served as a bowl alongside rice or Indian flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Dal, red gram (arhar), whole, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for cooked toor dal (red gram, whole, cooked) used as the primary basis. Dal Tadka is a tempered preparation of cooked lentils; the tempering (ghee, spices) adds modest fat and sodium. Values adjusted upward for fat (~+2 g/100 g) and sodium (~+150 mg/100 g) to reflect typical tadka preparation, consistent with ICMR-NIN compositional data for cooked legume dishes with added fat and salt.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Lentils, red, boiled (Canada)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dal Tadka is a spiced, tempered cooked lentil dish. GI of 29 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for red lentils, boiled (GI=29, low), as the closest analogous food. The tempering process does not substantially alter the glycemic response of the lentil base. GL per serving calculated as (29 × 14.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 10.5 g, based on 250 g serving and 14.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice tempering
hypertension.guidance: Dal Tadka contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (775 mg per 250 g serving), which represents about one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 29) and high dietary fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Tadka has a low glycemic index of approximately 29 and provides 3.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, supporting gradual glucose absorption; it is a suitable legume-based dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions alongside low-GI accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High fibre, plant protein, and moderate saturated fat from ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dal Tadka provides beneficial dietary fibre and plant-based protein that support cardiovascular health; however, the use of ghee contributes approximately 1.6 g saturated fat per 100 g, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should opt for preparations using minimal ghee or substitute with unsaturated oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Tadka is a classic North Indian lentil dish made from cooked yellow lentils — most commonly toor dal (split pigeon peas) or chana dal (split chickpeas) — that are finished with a hot tempering (tadka or tarka) of ghee or oil infused with cumin seeds, mustard seeds, garlic, dried red chillies, and sometimes onion and tomato. The lentils are first pressure-cooked or simmered until soft and creamy, then seasoned with turmeric, salt, and coriander. The sizzling tadka is poured over the cooked dal just before serving, releasing aromatic volatile compounds that define the dish's characteristic smoky, spiced fragrance. The texture is thick and soupy, with a smooth lentil base punctuated by the crunch of fried spices. Dal Tadka is a staple of everyday Indian home cooking and restaurant menus across the Indian subcontinent and the Indian diaspora worldwide, including Singapore. Nutritionally, it is a high-fibre, moderate-protein, low-glycemic-index dish well suited to individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. It provides plant-based protein (~7.2 g/100 g), dietary fibre (~3.8 g/100 g), iron, folate, and potassium. Sodium content is moderate due to added salt and should be monitored by hypertensive individuals. It is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian, though not vegan when prepared with ghee.
common_user_queries: Is dal tadka good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact Dal Tadka entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked toor dal data with a reasonable upward adjustment for fat and sodium to reflect the tadka tempering step. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (red lentils, boiled) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct Dal Tadka composite entry.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked red gram (toor dal) data; fat and sodium adjusted upward to account for ghee-based tadka tempering typical of restaurant preparation. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Dal Tadka is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients other than ghee, which contains negligible B12; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN plant food entries. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; ghee contains trace naturally occurring trans fats below detection threshold in standard reporting — confirmed trace/negligible per ICMR-NIN. 6. GI of 29 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (red lentils, boiled); the tempering process does not materially alter lentil GI. 7. The input record listed cuisine as 'Turkish' and food_group as 'Meat and alternatives'; both have been corrected — Dal Tadka is an Indian dish and a legume-based alternative, retained under 'Meat and alternatives' as legumes are classified there per standard food grouping. 8. The restaurant serving this dish (Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine) appears to offer Dal Tadka as a cross-cuisine menu item; the dish's canonical origin is Indian. 9. Multilingual names provided for Indian-origin dish served in Singapore context: Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi all populated. 10. Aliases consistency verified: all aliases_by_language string values appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
